title: Fire
summary: Starting, maintaining, and safely extinguishing fire in the wilderness.

subtopics.title: Starting Fire
subtopics.summary: Methods to start fire with and without modern tools.

articles.title: Bow Drill Technique
articles.content:
## Bow Drill Fire Starting

The bow drill is one of the most reliable primitive fire-starting methods.

## Materials Needed

- Fireboard (dry softwood like cedar, willow, or cottonwood)
- Spindle (same wood as fireboard, straight and dry)
- Bow (flexible branch with paracord or natural cordage)
- Handhold (hardwood with a depression to hold spindle)
- Tinder bundle (dry grass, cattail fluff, birch bark)

## Steps

- Carve a small depression in the fireboard and a notch leading to a collection point
- Create a socket in the handhold slightly larger than the spindle top
- String your bow with cord that has slight slack
- Loop the spindle once through the bowstring
- Position the spindle in the fireboard depression, apply downward pressure with handhold
- Bow back and forth in long, smooth strokes maintaining steady pressure
- Continue until smoke increases and a coal forms in the notch
- Tap the coal into your tinder bundle, fold the bundle, and blow gently until flames appear

## Tips for Success

- Wood must be completely dry — test by pressing thumbnail; it should not compress
- Keep strokes long and even, not fast and short
- Maintain consistent downward pressure throughout
- The notch should be cut to about 1/8 of the depression circle

**Warning:** Always prepare a fire site before starting — clear a 10-foot area of debris.

illustrations.caption: Bow drill kit: spindle, fireboard, bow, and hardwood handhold. All wood must be completely dry — test with thumbnail.
A glowing coal forms in the notch from friction heat. Tap carefully into tinder bundle and blow gently into flame.

articles.title: Flint and Steel
articles.content:
## Flint and Steel Fire Starting

One of the oldest reliable methods, requiring practice but very effective.

## Materials

- Hard rock (flint, chert, quartzite, or obsidian)
- High-carbon steel striker
- Char cloth or amadou (tinder fungus)
- Tinder bundle

## Steps

- Hold the flint firmly with your non-dominant hand, sharp edge facing upward
- Place char cloth on top of the flint to catch sparks
- Strike the steel downward along the flint edge at a 30-45 degree angle
- Sparks should land on the char cloth
- Once char cloth glows orange, fold it into your tinder bundle
- Blow gently until flames emerge

## Identifying Good Flint

- Glassy appearance
- Conchoidal (shell-like) fracture pattern
- Strikes produce bright sparks
- Found near riverbeds and limestone formations

illustrations.caption: Strike high-carbon steel at 30–45° down along the flint edge. Sparks reach over 2,000°F — direct them onto char cloth.
Good flint is glassy with a shell-like (conchoidal) fracture. Look for it near riverbeds and limestone outcrops.

articles.title: Fire Starting with Modern Tools
articles.content:
## Modern Fire Starting Tools

Always carry reliable fire-starting tools as backup to primitive methods.

## Priority Order

- Waterproof lighter (most reliable)
- Waterproof matches in sealed container
- Ferrocerium rod (firesteel) — works when wet
- Magnifying lens (only in sunlight)

## Ferrocerium Rod Technique

- Prepare dry tinder bundle first
- Hold the rod against the tinder, angle the scraper to push rod rather than pull scraper
- One firm scrape produces a shower of sparks at 3,000°F
- Direct sparks onto the finest, driest tinder

## Fire Lays

- **Teepee fire:** Sticks leaning inward in a cone — great for getting fire started quickly
- **Log cabin:** Stacked alternating logs — long-burning, good for cooking
- **Star fire:** Logs pushed in from 4 directions — easy to manage, conserves wood
- **Trench fire:** Dug into ground — reduces wind interference, harder to spot

**Always:** Never leave a fire unattended. Extinguish completely with water and check coals are cool before leaving.

illustrations.caption: Ferrocerium rod produces a shower of sparks at 3,000°F with a single firm scrape — works when wet, unlike matches.
Teepee fire lay: sticks leaning inward in a cone shape ignites quickly. Log cabin lay lasts longer for cooking.

subtopics.title: Fire Safety
subtopics.summary: Preventing wildfires and safely managing campfires.

articles.title: Extinguishing a Fire Completely
articles.content:
## How to Fully Extinguish a Campfire

A partially extinguished fire is one of the leading causes of wildfires.

## The 'Drown, Stir, Feel' Method

- **Drown:** Pour water slowly and thoroughly over all coals and embers
- **Stir:** Stir the ashes and embers with a stick while adding more water
- **Feel:** Place the back of your hand near (not in) the ash — should feel completely cold

## If No Water Available

- Scatter the ashes over a wide area
- Cover with dirt and stir thoroughly
- Never bury coals — they can smolder underground for days

## Check Before You Leave

- The fire pit should be cold to the touch
- No smoke, steam, or smell of burning
- Restore the site to its natural appearance

illustrations.caption: Pour water slowly while stirring. The back of your hand should feel completely cold — not just cool — before leaving.
Never bury coals in dirt — they can smolder underground for days and reignite. Scatter, then drench with water.

subtopics.title: Fire in Adverse Conditions
subtopics.summary: Starting and maintaining fire in wet, windy, and cold conditions.

articles.title: Starting Fire with Wet Wood
articles.content:
## Starting Fire with Wet Wood

Rain, dew, and moisture are the most common fire-starting obstacles in the wilderness.

## Finding Dry Fuel

- Look under dense conifer canopy — lower dead branches stay dry in rain
- Split logs expose dry inner wood even when the outside is soaked
- Dead wood under overhanging rocks or fallen logs stays drier than open ground
- Standing dead wood is drier than wood lying on the ground
- Inner bark of dead trees is often dry despite a wet outer surface

## Preparing Wet Tinder

- Fine shavings from dry inner wood catch faster than bark
- Birch bark contains oils that burn even when slightly damp
- Pine pitch nodules (hardened sap on trunk) ignite in almost any condition
- Cattail seed heads shed water and remain partially flammable
- Dry dead grass from the center of clumps or under leaf litter

## Fire Site Preparation

- Build a platform of dry sticks to keep the fire off wet ground
- A platform prevents moisture from wicking up into your fuel pile
- Build a windbreak on three sides using logs or rocks
- Prepare twice as much tinder and kindling as you think you need

## Techniques

- Start with the finest tinder and progress slowly to larger fuel
- Feather sticks: carve curling shavings that stay attached to the stick — large surface area catches easily
- Pine pitch firestarter: pack a ball of pitch around a twig end and light it — burns for several minutes even in rain
- Lean a large split log over the growing fire to pre-dry it before adding it as fuel

**Key rule:** Split everything. Wet wood is only wet on the outside.

articles.title: Fire in Wind and Cold
articles.content:
## Fire in Wind and Cold

Cold and wind drain heat from a fire faster than it can build. Site selection and technique determine success.

## Site Selection

- Use natural windbreaks: rock faces, dense brush, ravine walls, or snowbanks
- Never build directly against living trees
- In open terrain, dig a Dakota fire hole — nearly windproof and low-profile

## Dakota Fire Hole

- Dig a main chamber about 12 inches wide and 12 inches deep
- Dig a second angled tunnel connecting to the bottom of the first, opening upwind
- The intake tunnel creates a natural draft that feeds the fire with fresh air
- Works effectively in wind, produces less smoke, and is harder to spot
- Effective down to extreme cold with the right fuel

## Fuel Management in Cold

- Cold wood contains less volatile gas — requires more heat to ignite than warm wood
- Pre-warm small kindling by tucking it inside your jacket for a few minutes before use
- Gather three times more wood than you think you need before lighting
- Feed small pieces constantly in the early stages — resist adding large logs too early
- Resinous woods (pine, spruce, fir) ignite more easily in cold than hardwoods

## Protecting the Flame

- Cup hands around the tinder bundle while blowing to shield it from wind
- A piece of bark or a metal cup placed around the kindling blocks wind during ignition
- Once established, wet and cold fuel can be added gradually as the fire produces its own heat

## Troubleshooting

- Yellow flickering flame with lots of smoke: insufficient air or wet fuel
- Fire going out repeatedly: fuel added too fast before the fire is established
- Fix: reduce fuel size, allow more air, re-establish with dry fine tinder

**If a spot is not working after three attempts, move 10 feet and start fresh.**

title: Shelter
summary: Building emergency shelters to protect from exposure.

subtopics.title: Natural Shelters
subtopics.summary: Using the natural environment for quick shelter.

articles.title: Lean-To Shelter
articles.content:
## Building a Lean-To Shelter

The lean-to is the simplest and fastest emergency shelter to build.

## Site Selection

- Position with back to prevailing wind
- Avoid low spots where cold air and water collect
- Look up — remove dead branches (widow makers)
- Avoid lone tall trees in open areas (lightning risk)

## Construction

- Find two trees 7-8 feet apart
- Lash a ridgepole horizontally between the trees at shoulder height
- Lean branches/sticks at 45 degrees against the ridgepole
- Layer debris, bark, or pine boughs from bottom to top like shingles (4-6 inches thick)
- Build a debris floor 4-6 inches deep for insulation from cold ground

## Improving the Lean-To

- Add side walls at 45 degrees to block wind
- Build a fire reflector wall of green logs opposite the opening
- Size the shelter only as large as needed — smaller retains body heat better

**Key principle:** The shelter should be just big enough to hold you — every extra cubic foot of air takes more body heat to warm.

illustrations.caption: Position the lean-to back to prevailing wind. Avoid valley bottoms where cold air pools and lightning-prone lone trees.
Layer branches at 45° from ridgepole, overlapping like shingles from bottom up. Minimum 4–6 inches thick for rain protection.

articles.title: Debris Hut
articles.content:
## Building a Debris Hut

The debris hut can maintain a warm interior even without fire — relying on body heat.

## Construction

- Lay a ridgepole from ground to a fork or stump, long enough for your body plus 18 inches
- Create ribbing by leaning sticks along both sides of the ridgepole
- Fill in with smaller sticks and branches to create a lattice
- Pile debris (leaves, ferns, pine needles) on top — at least 3 feet thick on all sides
- Build a debris pile inside for bedding (at least 4 inches, ideally 12)
- Stuff the entrance with debris or your pack

## Sizing

- Width: just wide enough to roll over inside
- Length: just long enough to fit you fully inside
- Test: lie inside and check that you cannot sit up — if you can, it's too big

## Heat Retention

- A properly built debris hut will be 20-30°F warmer than outside
- Body heat alone is sufficient in most survival situations

illustrations.caption: Ridgepole from ground to a fork support. Ribs lean along both sides to form the lattice before debris piling begins.
Build small: if you can sit up inside, it's too big. Every extra cubic foot of air space steals your body heat.

subtopics.title: Winter Shelters
subtopics.summary: Snow caves, quinzhees, and cold-weather shelter techniques.

articles.title: Building a Snow Cave
articles.content:
## Building a Snow Cave

A snow cave can be 30–40°F warmer than outside air temperature and requires no equipment to build.

## Requirements

- A stable snow slope or drift at least 5–6 feet deep
- Snow that has settled and consolidated — not fresh powder
- A digging tool: stick, pot, or hands as a last resort

## Construction

- Select a slope of 30–45 degrees — steep enough to allow a raised sleeping platform inside
- Dig horizontally into the slope, then angle upward to hollow out an interior above the entrance
- Carve the interior dome-shaped to prevent drips from forming on a flat ceiling
- Create a raised sleeping platform higher than the entrance — cold air sinks and pools at the lower entrance level
- Poke a ventilation hole through the ceiling with a stick before sealing the entrance
- Keep the entrance small — just large enough to crawl through
- Mark the entrance with a ski, pole, or stick so rescuers or companions can find it after snowfall

## Interior Setup

- Keep all gear off the snow floor — use a sleeping pad, branches, or pack as insulation
- Leave your digging tool inside in case of collapse
- Never let interior temperature exceed 32°F — a melting interior weakens the structure
- Check ventilation hole regularly and keep it clear

## Recognizing Good Snow

- Good: firm, compacted, holds shape when squeezed like a snowball
- Poor: very powdery with no structure, or hard ice with visible layering that may collapse

**Always mark your snow cave entrance and ventilation hole. Rescuers have walked past occupied snow caves.**

articles.title: Building a Quinzhee
articles.content:
## Building a Quinzhee

A quinzhee is a hollowed snow mound — the best option when no suitable slope exists for a snow cave.

## Snow Cave vs. Quinzhee

- Snow cave: dug into an existing slope — faster, requires deep consolidated snow
- Quinzhee: pile and hollow — can be built on flat ground with any depth of snow, takes 2–4 hours

## Construction

- Pile snow into a dome approximately 8 feet wide and 6 feet tall
- Critical step: push 12–15 sticks (each 6–8 inches long) into the pile from all sides before hollowing — these are your wall thickness guides
- Allow the pile to sinter (harden) for at least 2 hours before hollowing — do not skip this
- Hollow out from the entrance, working inward and upward
- Stop digging when you hit a stick — that wall thickness is correct
- Remove each stick from the inside as you reach it
- Shape a domed interior with a raised sleeping platform
- Poke a ventilation hole in the ceiling with a stick

## Sintering

- Sintering is the bonding of snow crystals under their own weight over time
- Unsintered snow has little structural strength — the quinzhee will collapse while digging
- Very cold weather (below -10°F) requires longer sintering — up to 3 hours
- Test readiness: push your fist into the wall — it should resist, not sink

## Efficiency Tips

- Pile gear inside a tarp, cover with snow, then remove the gear — creates a ready-to-hollow interior
- Two people working speeds construction significantly
- Pack the exterior surface with a shovel after piling for added structural strength

**The sticks save your life. Never hollow a quinzhee without placing them first.**

subtopics.title: Improvised Shelters
subtopics.summary: Tarp, poncho, and quick-build shelter configurations.

articles.title: Tarp Shelter Configurations
articles.content:
## Tarp Shelter Configurations

A single 8×10 tarp can be rigged in over a dozen configurations. These four cover most survival situations.

## A-Frame

- String a ridgeline between two trees at shoulder height
- Drape the tarp over the ridgeline and stake all four corners to the ground
- Angle both sides at roughly 45° to shed rain
- Best all-around configuration: wind and rain protection on both sides
- Lower the ridgeline for better rain and wind resistance; raise it for more interior space

## Lean-To

- Attach the high edge to a ridgeline or two trees at chest height
- Stake the low edge to the ground angled away from you
- Open front allows a fire to radiate heat inside
- Best in calm, clear conditions — rain blows in from the open front

## Plow Point

- Stake one corner into the ground as the point
- Raise the opposite corner to a tree at head height
- Stake the remaining two corners out wide on each side
- Excellent rain shedding, low profile, strong in wind
- Orient the pointed end into the prevailing wind

## Ridgeline with Raised Floor

- Rig an A-frame but leave extra tarp material on each side
- Fold and stake the excess upward and outward to create raised edges
- Keeps the sleeping area dry when ground is wet or flooded

## General Tips

- Always orient the closed or lowest end toward the prevailing wind
- Pull ridgelines taut — a loose tarp flaps in wind and sheds rain poorly
- Guy-lines at 45° from each corner add stability in strong wind

**A well-pitched tarp provides equivalent protection to most tents in non-winter conditions.**

articles.title: Poncho Shelter
articles.content:
## Poncho Shelter

A military-style poncho is one of the most versatile emergency shelter materials available and is always on your body when you need it.

## Quick Lean-To

- Snap the hood shut
- Tie cordage through the corner grommets
- Attach two corners to trees or a ridgeline at chest height
- Stake the remaining two corners to the ground angled away from wind
- Can be erected in under 5 minutes

## A-Frame Hooch

- Attach a ridgeline through the hood opening or the center snap-loop
- Pull taut between two trees at waist height
- Stake all four corners to the ground at a 45° angle
- Smaller interior than a full tarp but complete rain protection on both sides

## Ground Sheet

- Spread flat beneath your sleeping area to block ground moisture and cold
- Snap two ponchos together for larger coverage with a partner

## Augmenting with Natural Materials

- Add debris walls on the sides of a poncho lean-to to block wind
- Lay pine boughs or debris over the poncho for extra insulation in cold
- A debris pile against the back of a poncho lean-to dramatically increases warmth retention

## Field Expedient Litter

- Snap two ponchos together with poles threaded through each side
- Creates a stretcher capable of carrying a casualty
- Poles can be cut branches, tent poles, or trekking poles

**Military-spec ponchos handle conditions that most cheap emergency ponchos cannot — worth the weight for extended trips.**

title: Water
summary: Finding, collecting, and purifying water in the wilderness.

subtopics.title: Finding Water
subtopics.summary: Locating water sources in various environments.

articles.title: Water Sources in the Wild
articles.content:
## Finding Water in the Wilderness

## The Rule of Threes

A person can survive approximately 3 days without water. Water is your top priority after immediate safety.

## Natural Indicators

- **Animal trails:** Converging trails often lead downhill to water
- **Birds:** Many birds fly to water at dawn and dusk — follow their direction
- **Insects:** Bees are always within 1 mile of water; mosquitoes indicate nearby standing water
- **Vegetation:** Willows, cottonwoods, cattails indicate water nearby
- **Topography:** Water collects in valleys, ravines, and at the base of cliffs

## Collecting Dew

- Tie absorbent cloth around ankles and walk through vegetation before sunrise
- Wring cloth into container
- Can collect significant amounts in humid areas

## Solar Still

- Dig a bowl-shaped hole in moist soil
- Place vegetation in the hole
- Put a container in the center
- Cover with plastic sheet, sealed at edges
- Place small stone in center to create a drip point
- Condensation drips into container — produces small amounts

## Snow and Ice

- Never eat snow directly — lowers core body temperature
- Melt snow before drinking
- Ice from frozen lakes is generally safer than snow (more compact)

illustrations.caption: Birds converge on water at dawn and dusk. Follow their flight direction. Bees are always within 1 mile of water.
Solar still: plastic sheet over a vegetation-filled pit, stone in center. Condensation drips into a container below.
Never eat snow directly — the energy to melt it lowers core body temperature. Always melt before drinking.

articles.title: Boiling Water
articles.content:
## Boiling Water for Safety

Boiling is the most reliable water purification method available without chemicals.

## How to Boil

- Bring water to a rolling boil (large bubbles)
- At elevations below 6,500 ft: boil for 1 minute
- At elevations above 6,500 ft: boil for 3 minutes (lower boiling point)
- Allow to cool before drinking

## Improvised Containers for Boiling

- Metal cans, bottles, or cooking pots
- Stone boiling: heat rocks in fire, drop into water-filled container
  - Use dense, non-porous rocks — avoid river rocks (can explode)
  - Use 8-10 hot rocks per liter of water

## What Boiling Kills

- All bacteria and viruses
- Giardia and Cryptosporidium
- Does NOT remove chemical contaminants, heavy metals, or salt

illustrations.caption: A rolling boil (large vigorous bubbles) is required — not just steaming. At altitude, boil for 3 minutes due to lower boiling point.
Stone boiling: heat 8–10 dense rocks in fire, drop into water container. Use non-porous rock — river rocks can explode.

articles.title: Improvised Water Filtration
articles.content:
## Improvised Water Filter

Filtration removes particles and some contaminants but is NOT sufficient alone — always combine with boiling or chemical treatment.

## Layered Filter Construction

From top to bottom in a container with a hole:

- Layer 1: Grass or cloth (removes large particles)
- Layer 2: Sand (removes small particles)
- Layer 3: Crushed charcoal from fire (removes chemicals, improves taste)
- Layer 4: Fine gravel
- Layer 5: Another grass/cloth layer

## Using Commercial Filters

- Squeeze filters (LifeStraw, Sawyer): filter directly while drinking
- Always follow manufacturer guidelines for replacement

## Chemical Treatment

- Iodine tablets: 1 tablet per liter, wait 30 minutes (ineffective against Cryptosporidium)
- Chlorine dioxide tablets: most effective chemical option, 4-hour wait for full effect
- Do not use if pregnant or have thyroid conditions

illustrations.caption: Layer filter from top: cloth → sand → crushed charcoal → gravel → cloth. Always follow with boiling or chemical treatment.
Chlorine dioxide tablets are the most effective chemical treatment — but require a 4-hour wait for full Cryptosporidium kill.

title: Navigation
summary: Finding your way with and without modern tools.

subtopics.title: Map Reading
subtopics.summary: Reading topographic maps and using grid references.

articles.title: Reading Topographic Maps
articles.content:
## Reading Topographic Maps

Topographic maps represent three-dimensional terrain in two dimensions using contour lines.

## Contour Lines

- Each line represents a constant elevation above sea level
- Contour interval: the elevation difference between adjacent lines, shown in the map legend
- Closely spaced lines = steep terrain
- Widely spaced lines = gentle or flat terrain
- Closed circles = hilltop (or depression if marked with inward-pointing tick marks)

## Identifying Terrain Features

- **Ridge:** Contour lines form a V or U shape pointing downhill
- **Valley:** Contour lines form a V pointing uphill — a stream flows in the bottom of the V
- **Saddle:** Two high points connected by a lower area — hourglass shape on the map
- **Cliff:** Lines so closely spaced they appear as a single thick line
- **Spur:** A ridge extending outward from higher ground — V pointing downhill

## Map Orientation

- Align the map so north on the map corresponds to north in the real world
- Use a compass or known landmarks to orient
- Once oriented, terrain features on the map match what you see in front of you

## Map Scale

- 1:24,000 (USGS standard): 1 inch = 2,000 feet — best detail for on-foot navigation
- 1:50,000: good for planning, less detail for trail navigation
- 1:100,000: suitable for vehicle routes, too coarse for hiking

## Measuring Distance

- Use the map's scale bar with a piece of string laid along your intended route
- Flat map distance underestimates actual travel time when significant elevation gain is involved

## Triangulation

- Identify two or three visible landmarks you can also locate on the map
- Take a bearing to each landmark with a compass
- Draw lines from each landmark on the map along the back-bearing (bearing + 180°)
- Where the lines intersect is your position

**Practice reading contour lines at home before relying on them in the field.**

articles.title: Grid References and Coordinates
articles.content:
## Grid References and Coordinate Navigation

## UTM vs. Latitude/Longitude

- **Latitude/Longitude:** Degrees, minutes, seconds — used by most consumer GPS devices and printed maps
- **UTM (Universal Transverse Mercator):** Grid in meters — easier to calculate ground distances
- Search and rescue teams typically use UTM; civilian hikers typically use lat/long

## Reading a 6-Digit Grid Reference

- Format: Easting (left to right) before Northing (bottom to top) — "read right, then up"
- First 3 digits = position left to right on the map
- Last 3 digits = position bottom to top on the map
- Accuracy: approximately 100 meters on the ground

## 8-Digit Grid Reference

- 4 digits Easting, 4 digits Northing
- Accuracy: approximately 10 meters
- Used for SAR and military coordination

## Communicating Your Position

- State the grid zone designator before coordinates when communicating with rescue services
- Confirm which datum is being used — WGS84 is the standard for all modern GPS devices
- Write down your GPS coordinates before entering areas with poor satellite reception

## GPS Navigation Basics

- Mark waypoints for camp, water sources, and trailhead before leaving the trailhead
- Track logs record where you have been — useful for backtracking accurately
- GPS accuracy degrades in deep canyons and under dense tree canopy
- Battery drain accelerates in cold — carry spare batteries or a power bank
- Always carry a paper map and compass as a backup to any electronic device

## Back-Azimuth

- The back-azimuth is the return bearing from your destination to your starting point
- Add 180° to your outbound bearing if it is less than 180°; subtract 180° if it is greater
- Example: depart on bearing 045°, return on bearing 225°

**Write your starting coordinates on paper before heading out — a dead GPS is useless without it.**

subtopics.title: Natural Navigation
subtopics.summary: Finding direction using the sun, stars, and natural indicators.

articles.title: Navigating by the Sun
articles.content:
## Navigating by the Sun

The sun is the most reliable daytime navigation tool available without equipment.

## Sun Arc Rules (Northern Hemisphere)

- The sun rises roughly in the east and sets roughly in the west — the exact point shifts with season
- At solar noon, the sun is due south and at its highest point in the sky
- In summer the sun tracks higher and further north; in winter it stays lower and further south

## Shadow Stick Method

- Push a straight stick (about 3 feet) vertically into level open ground
- Mark the tip of the shadow with a stone or twig (Mark 1)
- Wait 15–20 minutes
- Mark the new shadow tip (Mark 2)
- The line from Mark 1 to Mark 2 runs roughly west to east — Mark 1 is west, Mark 2 is east
- A perpendicular line through that east-west line points north and south
- Works at any time of day and in any season when the sun casts a shadow

## Watch Method (Analog Watch)

- In the Northern Hemisphere: point the hour hand directly at the sun
- The midpoint between the hour hand and the 12 o'clock mark points roughly south
- In the Southern Hemisphere: point the 12 o'clock mark at the sun; midpoint between 12 and the hour hand points north
- Works best when the sun is at a lower angle in the sky (morning and afternoon)

## Limitations

- Accuracy decreases near midday when the sun is near its peak
- Cloud cover that fully hides the sun eliminates these methods
- Seasonal variation means sunrise and sunset are not precisely east and west

## Maintaining Direction of Travel

- Pick a distant, fixed landmark in your intended direction of travel
- Walk toward it, then select the next landmark beyond it
- This prevents the natural tendency to veer gradually left or right over distance

**Combine with the shadow stick: take a bearing, walk for an hour, re-check alignment.**

articles.title: Navigating by Stars
articles.content:
## Navigating by Stars

Stars provide the most accurate natural navigation at night — they are fixed relative to true north, unlike a magnetic compass.

## Finding Polaris (Northern Hemisphere)

- Locate the Big Dipper (Ursa Major): seven bright stars in a dipper shape
- The two stars forming the outer edge of the cup are the pointer stars (Dubhe and Merak)
- Draw an imaginary line from the bottom pointer star through the top pointer star
- Extend that line approximately five times the distance between the two pointer stars
- The next moderately bright star in that direction is Polaris (the North Star)
- Polaris sits within 1° of true north at all times — it does not rise or set

## Confirming Polaris

- Polaris is the tip of the handle of the Little Dipper (Ursa Minor)
- It is the only star that does not appear to move during the night — all others rotate around it
- It is a moderately bright star, not the brightest in the sky (a common misconception)

## Southern Hemisphere: Southern Cross

- Locate the Southern Cross (Crux): four stars forming a distinct cross
- The long axis of the cross points toward the South Celestial Pole
- Extend the long axis approximately 4.5 times its own length to find the pole — no bright star marks it
- The two bright pointer stars (Alpha and Beta Centauri) nearby help confirm the cross's orientation

## Using Stars to Maintain Direction

- Identify Polaris, orient yourself, then choose a ground-level landmark in your direction of travel
- Re-check your bearing every 30–60 minutes as stars appear to move across the sky
- On a clear night, multiple reference stars on the horizon can serve as checkpoints

## Estimating Time

- Stars appear to rotate approximately 15° per hour around Polaris
- The Big Dipper acts as a rough clock hand sweeping counterclockwise around Polaris through the night

**Practice finding Polaris at home before you need it in the field.**

articles.title: Using a Compass
articles.content:
## Navigation with a Compass

## Parts of a Baseplate Compass

- Direction of travel arrow
- Rotating bezel with 360° markings
- Magnetic needle (red end points North)
- Orienting arrow (non-magnetic)
- Declination adjustment

## Taking a Bearing

- Point the direction of travel arrow at your target
- Rotate the bezel until the orienting arrow aligns with the magnetic needle (red in the shed)
- Read the bearing at the index line
- Walk in the direction of travel while keeping needle in orienting arrow

## Magnetic Declination

- Declination is the difference between true north and magnetic north
- In the US, varies from -20° to +20° by region
- Check your map or compass for local declination and adjust accordingly

## Without a Compass

- **Shadow stick method:** Place stick in ground, mark shadow tip, wait 15 min, mark again — line from first to second mark points roughly east
- **Watch method:** Point hour hand at sun; midpoint between hour hand and 12 is roughly south (northern hemisphere)
- **North Star (Polaris):** Find the Big Dipper, use the outer edge of the cup to point to Polaris — true north

illustrations.caption: Align the red magnetic needle over the orienting arrow (red in the shed). Read your bearing at the index line.
Shadow stick: mark two shadow tips 15 minutes apart. A line from first to second points roughly east — west is behind you.

articles.title: What to Do When Lost
articles.content:
## When You Are Lost: STOP

## The STOP Protocol

- **S — Stop:** Sit down. Panic causes bad decisions. Take a breath.
- **T — Think:** When did you last know where you were? What landmarks do you remember?
- **O — Observe:** Look for landmarks, water, trails, sun position. Listen for roads, water, people.
- **P — Plan:** Make a decision based on your assessment. Stay or move?

## Stay or Go?

**Stay if:**
- Someone knows your plan and expected return
- You are injured or with inexperienced group members
- Weather is deteriorating
- You have adequate water and shelter materials

**Move if:**
- No one knows your plans
- Water or shelter is unavailable at current location
- You can see a known landmark or hear civilization

## Signaling for Rescue

- Three of anything is the universal distress signal (whistles, fires, reflections)
- Signal mirror can be seen 10+ miles in sunlight
- Stay in open areas where you can be seen from aircraft
- Bright colors, smoke, and ground-to-air signals (large X or V in clearings)

**Always tell someone your plans before going out.**

illustrations.caption: STOP — Stop, Think, Observe, Plan. Sit down before doing anything. Panic leads to wandering further from help.
Signal mirror visible 10+ miles in sunlight. Flash toward aircraft — even if uncertain, keep flashing until it passes.

title: Food
summary: Finding and preparing food in the wilderness safely.

subtopics.title: Edible Plants
subtopics.summary: Identifying and safely eating wild plants.

articles.title: Universal Edibility Test
articles.content:
## Universal Edibility Test

Use this test when you cannot positively identify a plant and hunger is a serious concern. The process takes 24 hours.

## Before Starting

- Do not eat anything for 8 hours before the test
- Test only one plant part at a time (leaf, root, seed, fruit are separate tests)
- Have water available

## The 8-Step Process

- Step 1: Smell — Crush and smell. Strong unpleasant or almond odor = avoid
- Step 2: Skin contact — Rub plant juice on inner wrist, wait 15 minutes for reaction
- Step 3: Lip test — Touch plant to lips for 3 minutes. Any burning or tingling = stop
- Step 4: Tongue test — Place on tongue tip for 15 minutes without swallowing
- Step 5: Chew — Chew a small amount for 15 minutes without swallowing. Any reaction = spit out
- Step 6: Swallow a small amount — wait 8 hours
- Step 7: If no illness, eat a small handful — wait 8 hours
- Step 8: If still no illness, the plant part is likely edible

## Always Avoid

- White or yellow berries (80% are toxic)
- Plants with milky or discolored sap
- Plants with shiny leaves
- Spines, fine hairs, or thorns on the fruit
- Bitter or soapy taste
- Plants with umbrella-shaped flower clusters (many are deadly)

**Note:** The UET does not detect all toxins. Always prefer positively identified edible plants.

illustrations.caption: Skin contact test (Step 2): rub plant juice on the inner wrist. Any redness, burning, or swelling — do not proceed.
Avoid white, yellow, and red berries unless positively identified. 80% of white and yellow berries in temperate zones are toxic.

articles.title: Common Safe Wild Foods
articles.content:
## Reliably Identifiable Safe Wild Foods

## Cattail (Typha)

- Found near water; tall, cigar-shaped brown seed heads
- Edible parts: young shoots (raw/cooked), green seed heads (boiled), pollen (flour substitute), roots (starch)
- One of the most nutritious and identifiable wild foods

## Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale)

- Distinctive toothed leaves, hollow stems, yellow flowers
- All parts edible: leaves (salad or cooked), roots (roasted as coffee substitute), flowers (raw)
- Highest nutrition in young spring leaves

## Pine Trees (Pinus)

- Young green needles: steep in hot water for tea (high in Vitamin C)
- Inner bark (cambium): scrape and eat raw or cook
- Pine nuts (from certain species): high in calories and fat

## Clover (Trifolium)

- Distinctive three-leafed clusters
- Young leaves and flowers edible raw or cooked
- High in protein for a plant

## Acorns

- Must be leached to remove bitter tannins
- Crack, grind into paste, soak in cold water for 24 hours changing water every few hours
- Then use like flour or eat as mush

illustrations.caption: Cattails: tall plants with a distinctive brown cigar-shaped seed head. Found near water — edible shoots, pollen, and starchy roots.
Pine needle tea is high in Vitamin C — steep young green needles in hot water. Inner bark (cambium) is also edible.
Acorns must be leached: grind, soak in cold water 24 hours changing water every few hours to remove bitter tannins.

title: Signaling
summary: Attracting rescuers and communicating your position.

subtopics.title: Fire & Smoke Signals
subtopics.summary: Using fire and smoke to attract rescuers by day and night.

articles.title: Building a Signal Fire
articles.content:
## Building a Signal Fire

A signal fire visible from aircraft requires preparation before you hear the aircraft — not after.

## Site Selection

- Open areas only: clearings, hilltops, lakeshores, beaches, ridgelines
- Avoid tree cover that blocks visibility from the air
- Higher elevation increases the distance from which a fire is visible
- Prepare at least two signal fire sites in different directions to cover varying aerial approaches

## Pre-Built Fire Lay

- Build a teepee fire lay for rapid ignition
- Prepare three signal fires in a triangle formation, 25–50 meters apart — three fires is the international distress signal
- Cover pre-built fires with bark or boughs to keep tinder dry
- Have a lit tinder bundle ready so fires can be ignited within 30 seconds of hearing an aircraft

## Fuel for Maximum Visibility

- Dry softwood produces tall, fast-starting flames — use as the base fuel
- Resinous wood (pine, spruce, fir) burns with visible dark smoke
- Keep a reserve fuel pile beside each fire for sustained burning
- Prepare green vegetation separately for smoke production during daylight

## When to Light

- Light when you have confirmed the aircraft is approaching, not just in the area
- Do not ignite all three fires simultaneously on the first sound — confirm direction first
- Maintain fires for at least 30 minutes after an aircraft passes — it may be notifying others and circling back

## Night Signal Fires

- Flames are far more visible at night than smoke
- Maximize flame height using dry split wood
- Avoid green vegetation at night — it produces steam and suppresses flame height
- A fire visible to a pilot at 1,000 feet altitude can be seen from 15+ miles in clear conditions

**Build your signal fires as the first task after your shelter is secured.**

articles.title: Smoke Signals
articles.content:
## Smoke Signals

Smoke is most effective for daytime signaling. Color contrast against the background determines visibility.

## Smoke Color vs. Background

- **Black smoke:** most visible against sky, snow, and light-colored terrain — use rubber, oil, or green vegetation over fire
- **White smoke:** most visible against dark backgrounds like forest or dark rock — use damp green leaves, wet moss, or grass
- General rule: use black smoke in open or snowy terrain; use white smoke in forested areas

## Producing Black Smoke

- Add green leafy vegetation over an established fire
- Wet leaves layered over burning dry wood produce dark smoke reliably
- Rubber or plastic (from vehicle tires or gear) produces thick black smoke visible for miles — use only if no other option
- Pine boughs over a hot fire produce dark, visible smoke

## Producing White Smoke

- Bundle damp green ferns or moss over flames after the fire is established
- Wet evergreen boughs draped over a fire create white-grey smoke
- Dense grass matted over burning coals creates sustained white smoke output

## The Blanket Method

- Once smoke is established, repeatedly cover and uncover the fire with a tarp, space blanket, or jacket
- Produce three distinct puffs with pauses between — the universal smoke distress pattern
- Consistent puffs attract more attention than a continuous column

## Atmospheric Conditions

- Smoke rises less in cold, calm, humid conditions and stays low — less visible from aircraft
- In hot, dry, windy conditions smoke rises and disperses quickly — increase fuel to compensate
- Build signal fires on high ground to help smoke rise above terrain and tree level

## Night Signaling

- Smoke is invisible at night — switch to maximum flame height
- Save green vegetation for daytime smoke; use dry split wood for night flames

**In calm conditions, smoke from a signal fire can be visible to aircraft at altitude from over 50 miles away.**

articles.title: Ground-to-Air Signals
articles.content:
## Ground-to-Air Signaling

## Standard Symbols (use at least 10 feet in size)

- **X** — Need medical assistance
- **V** — Need help / rescue
- **→** — Traveling in this direction
- **F** — Need food and water
- **LL** — All is well
- **SOS** — International distress

## Construction

- Use high-contrast materials: rocks on snow, logs on sand, bright clothes
- Place in open areas visible from air
- Trample vegetation to create signals visible from above

## Signal Mirror

- Can be seen 10+ miles away in direct sunlight
- Aim reflection by holding mirror near eye, create a V-shape with fingers at arm's length, put target in V
- Flash toward aircraft even if unsure if seen

## Three-Signal Rule

- Three whistle blasts, three fires in triangle, three gunshots = universal distress signal
- Space signals 1 minute apart, repeat every 3 minutes

illustrations.caption: X = need medical help, V = need rescue, SOS = international distress. Make symbols at least 10 ft wide for aerial visibility.
illustrations.afterHeading: Standard Symbols (use at least 10 feet in size)

illustrations.caption: Three of anything signals distress worldwide — three whistle blasts, three fires in a triangle, three gunshots.

title: Tools & Knots
summary: Essential knots, cordage, and improvised tools.

subtopics.title: Lashing Techniques
subtopics.summary: Binding poles and branches to build shelters, stretchers, and tools.

articles.title: Square and Diagonal Lashing
articles.content:
## Square and Diagonal Lashing

Lashing lets you build strong structures without nails using only poles and cordage.

## Materials

- Paracord, natural cordage (inner bark, dogbane, stinging nettle), or rawhide
- Straight, dry poles or branches
- Pre-stretch all cordage before use on critical structures

## Square Lashing

Used to join two poles at a right angle (90°).

- Tie a clove hitch around one pole at the intersection
- Wrap over the crosspiece, under the main pole, over the other side, and back — 3–4 full wraps
- Take frapping turns between the poles (wrap in the gap, pulling each tight) — 2–3 fraps
- Finish with a clove hitch on the crosspiece
- Test: grip the lashing — poles should not rotate or shift

## Diagonal Lashing

Used when poles cross at angles other than 90°, or when poles spring apart under tension.

- Tie a timber hitch diagonally around both poles to draw them together
- Wrap 3–4 turns on each diagonal axis, forming an X pattern
- Take 2–3 frapping turns between the poles
- Finish with a clove hitch
- Better than square lashing for X-bracing under load

## Common Mistakes

- Under-tensioning wraps — each wrap must be pulled firm, not just the last one
- Skipping frapping turns — fraps are what prevent rotation, not the wraps
- Using a clove hitch to start diagonal lashing — use a timber hitch to pull poles together first

## Uses

- Square lashing: shelters, ladders, stretcher frames, tool extensions
- Diagonal lashing: X-bracing, bipod supports, angled structures

**Practice until you can complete either lashing in under 2 minutes.**

articles.title: Shear and Tripod Lashing
articles.content:
## Shear and Tripod Lashing

Shear and tripod lashings create multi-leg structures that spread load and stand independently.

## Shear Lashing

Used to join two parallel poles end-to-end to extend length, or to create an A-frame.

- Lay two poles parallel side by side
- Tie a clove hitch on one pole
- Wrap loosely around both poles together: 6–8 wraps
- Take 2–3 frapping turns in the gap between the poles
- Finish with a clove hitch on the second pole
- Splay the poles apart to form the A-frame — wraps allow rotation and tighten as load is applied

## Shear Lashing Applications

- A-frame uprights for a ridgepole
- Extending a pole beyond its natural length
- Stretcher side rails spliced from shorter poles

## Tripod Lashing

Creates a three-legged self-standing structure.

- Lay three poles parallel with the middle pole head facing opposite the outer two
- Tie a clove hitch on one outer pole
- Wrap loosely around all three poles together: 6–8 wraps
- Frap between each pair of poles: 2–3 turns between each gap (two frapping sequences)
- Finish with a clove hitch on the last pole
- Stand the tripod by splaying the legs — the lashing tightens under load

## Tripod Applications

- Cooking tripod over a fire
- Hanging a water filter or bag
- Signal platform
- Anchor point for hauling gear

## Load Testing

- Always test a lashed structure before committing weight to it
- Apply gradual load while checking for slippage at the lashing points
- A properly built shear-lashed shelter frame will support 200+ lbs

**The frapping turns do the real work — never rush or skip them.**

articles.title: 10 Essential Knots
articles.content:
## Essential Wilderness Knots

## Square Knot

- Use: Joining two ropes of equal diameter
- Right over left, then left over right
- Easy to remember: 'right over left and under; left over right and under'

## Bowline

- Use: Loop that won't slip; rescue, attaching to anchors
- 'The rabbit comes out of the hole, around the tree, and back in the hole'
- Makes a non-tightening loop — safe for attaching around the body

## Clove Hitch

- Use: Quickly securing rope to a pole or branch
- Two loops in the same direction, second loop tucked under itself
- Good for starting lashings

## Taut-Line Hitch

- Use: Adjustable tent guyline, adjustable loops
- Grips under tension, slides when released

## Figure-Eight

- Use: Stopper knot at end of rope; figure-eight on a bight for loops
- Most reliable stopper knot

## Half Hitch (and Two Half Hitches)

- Use: Quick securing, beginning of many other knots
- Two half hitches around a post = very secure temporary tie

## Sheet Bend

- Use: Joining two ropes of DIFFERENT diameters
- Works where square knot fails

## Timber Hitch

- Use: Dragging logs, attaching rope to rough timber
- Tightens as load increases

## Trucker's Hitch

- Use: Creating mechanical advantage to tighten lashings
- Creates a 3:1 mechanical advantage

## Prusik Hitch

- Use: Ascending rope, safety on rappel
- Small loop grips main rope under load, slides when unweighted

illustrations.caption: Square knot: right over left then left over right. Only use to join two ropes of equal diameter — not for safety-critical loads.
Bowline creates a fixed, non-slip loop — safe for rescue and body attachment. Easy to untie even after heavy loading.
Clove hitch: two same-direction loops, second tucked under itself. Quick to tie on poles — good starting point for lashings.

title: Wildlife & Hazards
summary: Staying safe around dangerous animals and plants in the wilderness.

subtopics.title: Large Animal Encounters
subtopics.summary: Preventing and responding to encounters with bears, mountain lions, and moose.

articles.title: Bear Safety
articles.content:
## Bear Safety

Most bear encounters are preventable. The few that are not require a clear, immediate response.

## Prevention

- Make noise while hiking — talk, clap, or use a bear bell in dense brush
- Never hike alone in known bear country
- Stay on marked trails where possible — bears avoid high-traffic areas
- Keep dogs on leash; a fleeing dog often leads a bear back to you

## Food and Scent Management

- Bear hang: suspend food, trash, and scented items at least 10 feet high and 4 feet from the trunk, 200 feet from camp
- Bear canister: required in many national parks — more reliable than a hang
- Never cook or eat in your tent
- Change clothes worn while cooking before sleeping
- Pack out all trash — do not bury food scraps

## Encounter Response

- Stay calm and do not run — running triggers a predatory chase response
- Speak calmly in a low voice to identify yourself as human
- Make yourself look larger: raise arms, open jacket, group together
- Back away slowly while facing the bear — never turn your back

## Bear Spray

- Most effective deterrent available — more reliable than firearms in most studies
- Effective range: 20–30 feet; deploy only when bear is within range and charging
- Aim slightly downward to create a cloud the bear runs into
- Keep accessible on your hip, not inside your pack

## Black Bear Attack

- Black bear attacks are almost always predatory — fight back aggressively
- Target the nose and eyes
- Do not play dead with a black bear

## Grizzly/Brown Bear Attack

- If a grizzly makes contact after a surprise encounter: play dead — face down, hands laced over neck, legs spread to resist being flipped
- If a grizzly attacks in your camp at night or has been stalking you: this is predatory — fight back aggressively
- Maintain the play-dead position until the bear has fully left the area

**Know which bear species inhabit your area before you go out.**

articles.title: Mountain Lions, Moose, and Other Large Animals
articles.content:
## Mountain Lions

Mountain lion attacks are rare but almost always predatory. The correct response is the opposite of a bear encounter.

## Mountain Lion Encounter

- Do not run — it activates predatory instinct
- Face the animal and maintain eye contact
- Make yourself appear as large as possible: raise arms, open jacket, lift children immediately
- Speak loudly and firmly
- Back away slowly without turning your back
- If attacked: fight back immediately and aggressively — target eyes and nose
- Never play dead with a mountain lion

## Signs of Mountain Lion Presence

- Tracks: round pad, 3–4 inches wide, no claw marks (retractable claws)
- Cached prey: a carcass covered with debris and leaves nearby
- Claw marks on trees at head height
- If you find a cached kill, leave the area immediately — the lion is likely watching

## Moose

Moose injure more people annually in North America than bears. They are large, fast, and unpredictable.

## Moose Warning Signs

- Ears laid flat
- Hair raised on neck and shoulders
- Head lowered and swinging side to side
- Licking lips repeatedly
- Any of these signs: increase distance immediately — a moose charge is not a bluff

## Moose Encounter

- Get behind a large solid object — a tree, boulder, or vehicle
- A moose will kick and stomp but will not chase long distances
- If knocked down: curl into a ball, protect your head, and stay down until it stops and moves away
- Never position yourself between a cow moose and her calf

## Other Animals

- **Bison:** Never approach — can run 35 mph and turn instantly; stay at least 75 feet away
- **Elk during rut (Sept–Oct):** Bulls are aggressive; give a wide berth and do not linger near them
- **Wild boar:** Nocturnal, poor eyesight; if encountered unexpectedly, stand still or climb a tree

**Most large animal encounters end without incident if you do not run and do not approach.**

subtopics.title: Venomous Animals
subtopics.summary: Identifying and responding to bites and stings from venomous animals.

articles.title: Venomous Snakes
articles.content:
## Venomous Snakes

In North America, venomous snakes fall into two groups: pit vipers and coral snakes.

## Pit Vipers (Rattlesnakes, Copperheads, Cottonmouths)

- Triangular, arrow-shaped head wider than the neck
- Heat-sensing pit between the eye and nostril
- Vertical, elliptical pupils
- Rattlesnakes: rattle at tail tip — young snakes may not rattle
- Copperhead: hourglass-shaped bands, copper-colored head
- Cottonmouth: thick body, white inner mouth visible when threatened, found near water

## Coral Snakes

- Slender body, small round head, round pupils
- Bright red, yellow, and black banding
- North American rhyme: 'Red touches yellow, kill a fellow; red touches black, friend of Jack'
- This rhyme does not apply outside North America — learn local species

## Avoiding Snake Bites

- Watch where you place hands and feet — never reach into crevices or under logs without looking first
- Step on top of a log before stepping over it — snakes shelter on the far side
- Wear boots and long pants in snake country
- Most bites occur when people attempt to handle or kill a snake

## If Bitten

- Stay calm — elevated heart rate spreads venom faster
- Remove rings, watches, and tight clothing near the bite before swelling begins
- Keep the bitten limb at or below heart level
- Note the time of the bite and mark the edge of swelling with a pen every 15 minutes to track progression
- Move to evacuation immediately — antivenom is the only effective treatment
- Do NOT: cut and suck, apply a tourniquet, apply ice, use a venom extractor device

**Over 80% of snake bites are on hands and forearms — from people who tried to touch the snake.**

articles.title: Spiders, Scorpions, and Centipedes
articles.content:
## Black Widow

- Shiny black body with a red hourglass marking on the underside
- Found under rocks, in woodpiles, outhouses, and dark undisturbed spaces
- Bite often painless initially — pain develops over 30–60 minutes
- Symptoms: spreading muscle cramps, abdominal rigidity, sweating, elevated heart rate
- Treatment: remain calm, immobilize the limb, seek medical care — antivenom is available

## Brown Recluse

- Tan to dark brown body with a violin-shaped marking on the back
- Six eyes arranged in pairs (most spiders have eight)
- Found in dry undisturbed areas: under bark, in folded clothing, in shoes
- Bite often painless initially — a necrotic ulcer may develop over several days
- Treatment: clean the wound, seek medical care, monitor closely for expanding tissue damage

## Prevention (Spiders and Scorpions)

- Shake out clothing, shoes, and sleeping bags before putting them on — establish this as a daily habit
- Do not place hands in crevices or under rocks without looking first
- Check inside boots every morning in spider and scorpion country

## Scorpions

- Most North American scorpions produce only mild, localized symptoms
- Exception: the Arizona bark scorpion (pale yellow, thin claws) is medically significant
- Dangerous symptoms: numbness and tingling spreading from the sting, muscle twitching, difficulty swallowing — children are at greatest risk
- Treatment: seek emergency care for bark scorpion stings, especially in children

## Centipedes

- Large centipedes (Scolopendra) deliver a painful bite — rarely dangerous in healthy adults
- Typical symptoms: local pain, swelling, and redness
- Clean the wound and monitor for secondary infection

**Establish a shake-out routine for all gear and clothing every morning when camping in arid or warm regions.**

subtopics.title: Insects, Ticks, and Dangerous Plants
subtopics.summary: Managing ticks, stinging insects, and toxic plant exposure.

articles.title: Ticks and Stinging Insects
articles.content:
## Ticks

Ticks transmit Lyme disease, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, and other serious illnesses. Prevention and prompt removal limit risk significantly.

## Prevention

- Wear long sleeves and pants tucked into socks in tick habitat: tall grass, leaf litter, and forest edges
- Use DEET or permethrin-treated clothing in high-risk areas
- Walk in the center of trails — ticks climb to the tips of vegetation and wait, they do not jump or fly
- Do a full-body tick check within 2 hours of returning from outdoors; pay attention to hairline, behind ears, armpits, and behind knees

## Tick Removal

- Use fine-tipped tweezers — grip the tick as close to the skin surface as possible
- Pull upward with steady, even pressure — do not twist or jerk
- Do not crush the tick body with your fingers
- Clean the bite area with rubbing alcohol or soap and water after removal
- Note the date; save the tick in a sealed bag if you want it tested later

## Do NOT Do

- Do not use petroleum jelly, nail polish, heat, or a lit match to remove a tick — these methods cause the tick to regurgitate into the wound, increasing disease risk

## Warning Signs After a Tick Bite

- An expanding ring-shaped (bull's-eye) rash around the bite site — seek medical care immediately
- Fever, chills, or flu-like symptoms within 2–3 weeks of a bite — inform the doctor about the tick bite

## Bees, Wasps, and Hornets

- Remove a bee stinger immediately by scraping sideways with a fingernail or card — do not pinch and pull
- Normal reaction: local pain, swelling, redness — treat with a cold pack and oral antihistamine
- Anaphylaxis warning signs: hives spreading beyond the sting site, throat tightening, difficulty breathing, dizziness — use epinephrine (EpiPen) immediately and evacuate
- If a nest is disturbed: run in a straight line away from the nest; do not swat; seek shelter indoors or in a vehicle

**Carry an EpiPen if you have a known insect sting allergy — anaphylaxis can be fatal within minutes.**

articles.title: Dangerous Plants
articles.content:
## Poison Ivy, Oak, and Sumac

All three produce urushiol — an oil that causes an allergic rash in roughly 85% of people.

- **Poison ivy:** Three leaflets ('leaves of three, let it be'), shiny when young, white berries, grows as a ground cover or climbing vine
- **Poison oak:** Three leaflets with rounded lobes resembling oak leaves, common on the US West Coast
- **Poison sumac:** 7–13 leaflets on a single stem, white berries, found in wetlands

## Urushiol Exposure

- Rash appears 12–72 hours after contact — it is not spread by scratching, only by urushiol transfer
- Wash exposed skin with soap and cold water within 10 minutes to significantly reduce severity
- Wash all gear and clothing that contacted the plant
- Never burn these plants — inhaling urushiol smoke causes severe internal airway inflammation
- Treatment: hydrocortisone cream for mild cases; see a doctor for widespread or facial rash

## Deadly Plants

- **Water hemlock (Cicuta):** Most violently toxic plant in North America — resembles wild carrot, found near water, umbrella-shaped white flowers; avoid all plants in this family unless certain of identification
- **Deadly nightshade:** Shiny black berries, purple bell-shaped flowers — small amounts are dangerous
- **Monkshood (Aconite):** Purple hood-shaped flowers in mountain meadows — toxic through skin contact and ingestion
- **Death camas:** Closely resembles wild onion but has no onion smell — bulb and leaves are deadly

## Field Rules

- Do not eat any plant with umbrella-shaped flower clusters
- Do not eat any plant with white or yellow berries unless positively identified
- Do not eat any plant that produces milky or discolored sap when broken
- If uncertain, do not eat it

**Urushiol remains active on unwashed gear and clothing for years — rashes from old exposure are common.**

title: Weather
summary: Reading weather signs, staying safe in storms, and managing temperature extremes.

subtopics.title: Reading the Sky
subtopics.summary: Using clouds, wind, and natural signs to predict weather changes.

articles.title: Cloud Reading and Storm Prediction
articles.content:
## Cloud Reading and Storm Prediction

Clouds are the most reliable short-range weather indicator available without instruments.

## Cloud Types

- **Cirrus:** Thin, wispy, high-altitude — fair weather if isolated; approaching front if increasing and thickening over hours
- **Cumulus:** Puffy, flat-bottomed, white — fair weather when small; if they build tall through the afternoon, storms possible by evening
- **Cumulonimbus:** Tall anvil-topped thunderhead — storm is here or arriving within the hour
- **Stratus:** Low, grey, featureless layer — overcast and drizzle; rarely violent
- **Altocumulus:** Mid-level rippled or wavy layer — mackerel sky; often precedes a front within 24 hours

## Rapid Development Warning Signs

- Cumulus clouds building rapidly upward during the morning
- Sky darkening in the west
- Sudden temperature drop with increasing wind
- Distant rumbling or flashes
- Any combination of the above: plan to be in shelter within 30 minutes

## Wind Direction

- In the Northern Hemisphere: wind shifting from south to west to north typically signals a clearing front
- Wind shifting from north to east to south typically signals an approaching storm
- A sudden gust from one direction often precedes a squall line

## Barometric Pressure

- Falling pressure = worsening weather
- Rising pressure = improving conditions
- A pressure drop of more than 3 hPa per hour is a strong storm warning — some GPS watches and altimeters include a barometer

## Mountain Weather

- Weather moves faster in mountains — apply a larger safety margin than the forecast suggests
- Afternoon thunderstorms are common and predictable in mountain terrain during summer
- Clear mornings are typical even when afternoon storms are certain — do not be misled

**In the mountains, plan to be at or below treeline by noon during summer thunderstorm season.**

articles.title: Natural Weather Indicators
articles.content:
## Natural Weather Indicators

Animals, plants, and atmospheric conditions all provide advance warning of weather changes.

## Animal Behavior

- Birds flying low or roosting early — low pressure approaching, storm likely
- Swallows and swifts flying high — fair weather; flying low — humidity rising before rain
- Frogs calling more loudly than usual — humidity rising, rain likely
- Bees returning to the hive mid-afternoon on a clear day — storm may be approaching

## Plant Indicators

- Clover folds its leaflets before rain
- Dandelion and daisy flowers close before rain
- Pine cones open in dry air, close when humidity rises significantly

## Atmospheric Signs

- **Red sky at night:** High pressure and fair weather likely — reliable in mid-latitudes
- **Red sky in the morning:** Moisture-laden low pressure may be approaching — warrants attention
- **Halo around the moon or sun:** Thin cirrus clouds at altitude — a front may arrive within 24 hours
- **Heavy dew on grass in the morning:** Clear night with high pressure — generally good weather ahead
- **No dew when expected:** Cloud cover or wind prevented overnight cooling — conditions may be changing

## Sound and Smell

- Petrichor (earthy smell before rain): rising humidity causes soil bacteria to release geosmin
- Sounds carrying farther than usual: sound travels better in high-humidity, low-pressure air
- Campfire smoke descending to the ground rather than rising: low pressure — weather is deteriorating

## Building a Weather Picture

- No single indicator is reliable alone — look for multiple signs pointing the same direction
- Increasing cloud, descending campfire smoke, low-flying insects, and rising humidity together are a strong combined signal
- Note conditions at the same time each morning and evening to track trends over time

**Local weather patterns vary by geography — learn the specific signs for the area you frequent.**

subtopics.title: Lightning and Storms
subtopics.summary: Avoiding and surviving lightning strikes and severe storm events.

articles.title: Lightning Safety
articles.content:
## Lightning Safety

Lightning kills more people annually in the US than tornadoes or hurricanes. Most deaths are preventable.

## The 30-30 Rule

- If the time between lightning and thunder is 30 seconds or less, the storm is within 6 miles — seek shelter immediately
- After the last lightning, wait 30 full minutes before returning to exposed terrain
- Sound travels approximately 1 mile per 5 seconds — count the seconds between flash and thunder and divide by 5

## Safe Shelter

- Best: a substantial building with plumbing or electrical wiring, which grounds the structure
- Good: a hard-topped metal vehicle with windows fully closed
- Not safe: open shelters, picnic pavilions, isolated trees, convertibles, or any open vehicle

## What to Avoid

- Hilltops, ridgelines, and open summits
- Isolated tall trees — lightning preferentially strikes the tallest nearby object
- Open water — get off lakes and rivers immediately when a storm approaches
- Open fields where you are the highest object
- Shallow caves and rock overhangs — ground current can arc across the opening
- Fences, wire, and railway tracks — conduct electricity over long distances

## If Caught in the Open

- Move to a low-lying area away from isolated trees
- Spread your group out at least 50 feet apart to prevent multiple casualties from a single strike
- Lightning crouch: feet together, squat on the balls of your feet, hands over ears, head down — do not lie flat (ground current travels along the surface)
- Put down metal-framed packs, trekking poles, and umbrellas

## After a Strike

- A struck person carries no electrical charge — it is safe to touch them immediately
- Begin CPR if there is no pulse — lightning strike survivors have a high survival rate with prompt CPR
- Treat for burns at both the entry and exit points

**In the mountains, be descending from exposed terrain before noon during summer months.**

articles.title: Flash Floods and Severe Storms
articles.content:
## Flash Floods

Flash floods can strike a canyon or wash even when the sky above you is clear — the rain falls miles away upstream.

## High-Risk Terrain

- Slot canyons and narrow gorges: no escape routes, extremely rapid water rise
- Desert washes and dry creek beds: drain vast areas of rock and hard soil with no absorption
- River campsites during storm season: rivers can rise several feet in minutes
- Low-water crossings during or after rain events

## Warning Signs

- Thunderstorms visible upstream or uphill — even storms that appear distant
- A sudden increase in water flow volume or a color change to brown or muddy
- A roaring or rumbling sound coming from upstream
- Floating debris or vegetation appearing in a previously clear channel
- Water rising while you are in the middle of a stream crossing

## Response

- Never camp in a wash, dry riverbed, or at a low river bank, regardless of forecast
- If any warning sign is present: move to high ground immediately — do not wait to confirm the flood
- If caught in a flood: move perpendicular to the current toward high ground; do not try to outrun water downstream
- Do not attempt to cross a flowing wash on foot if water is above knee level or moving fast
- In a vehicle: just 12 inches of moving water can sweep a car off a road — turn around, do not drive through

## Tornado Warning Signs

- Dark greenish sky
- Large hail preceding or accompanying the storm
- A continuous deep roar unlike thunder
- A visible funnel or rotating debris cloud
- If caught outdoors: lie flat in a low-lying ditch or depression; protect your head with both arms; stay away from trees and vehicles

**Always check flood and weather forecasts before entering canyon or desert terrain, regardless of current local conditions.**

subtopics.title: Extreme Temperatures
subtopics.summary: Recognizing and treating hypothermia, frostbite, heat exhaustion, and heat stroke.

articles.title: Hypothermia and Frostbite
articles.content:
## Hypothermia

Hypothermia occurs when core body temperature drops below 95°F (35°C). It can develop at temperatures well above freezing when wet or wind-exposed.

## Risk Factors

- Wet clothing — water conducts heat away from the body 25× faster than still air
- Wind — dramatically accelerates heat loss even in mild temperatures
- Exhaustion and dehydration — reduce the body's ability to generate and conserve heat
- Most hypothermia deaths in North America occur between 30°F and 50°F

## Stages and Signs

- **Mild (95–90°F):** Shivering, impaired coordination, slurred speech, poor judgment
- **Moderate (90–82°F):** Shivering stops, muscle rigidity, severe confusion, drowsiness
- **Severe (below 82°F):** Unconscious, very slow or absent pulse, blue or puffy skin — person may appear dead

## Treatment

- Get the person out of wind, rain, and cold — remove all wet clothing
- Insulate from the ground using sleeping pads, packs, or clothing
- Cover the head — significant heat loss occurs through an uncovered head
- Provide warm (not hot) sweet fluids if the person is conscious and able to swallow
- Skin-to-skin contact inside a sleeping bag is effective for mild to moderate cases
- Do not rub the extremities vigorously — this drives cold blood toward the core
- Evacuate all moderate and severe cases — cardiac arrhythmia is the primary danger

## Frostbite

Frostbite occurs when tissue freezes — typically affects fingers, toes, nose, ears, and cheeks.

- **Frostnip:** Skin white and waxy, numb but soft when pressed — rewarm with body heat (armpit or groin)
- **Superficial frostbite:** Hard surface, soft underneath, blisters form after rewarming
- **Deep frostbite:** Tissue hard all the way through, blue or black color

## Frostbite Treatment

- Do not rewarm if there is any chance of refreezing — refreezing causes far greater tissue damage than remaining frozen
- Rewarm in water at 99–102°F until tissue becomes soft and flushed
- Do not rub, massage, or walk on frostbitten feet
- Protect blistered tissue and evacuate

**Hypothermia impairs judgment — affected individuals often resist treatment and may attempt to remove clothing.**

articles.title: Heat Exhaustion and Heat Stroke
articles.content:
## Heat Illness Spectrum

Heat illness progresses from cramps through exhaustion to life-threatening heat stroke. Recognizing the stages early is critical.

## Heat Cramps

- Painful muscle spasms in legs, arms, or abdomen during or after exertion in heat
- Caused by salt and fluid loss through heavy sweating
- Treatment: rest in shade, drink fluids with electrolytes, gently stretch and massage the affected muscle

## Heat Exhaustion

The body is struggling to cool itself but core temperature remains near normal.

- Symptoms: heavy sweating, pale and clammy skin, weakness, nausea, headache, dizziness, fast weak pulse
- Treatment:
  - Move to shade or a cool environment immediately
  - Lay the person down with feet slightly elevated
  - Remove excess clothing
  - Apply cool wet cloths to skin, especially neck, armpits, and groin
  - Give cool water or an electrolyte drink if the person is conscious
  - If no improvement within 15 minutes, treat as heat stroke and evacuate

## Heat Stroke

A life-threatening emergency — core temperature above 104°F (40°C). The body's cooling system has failed.

- **Classic heat stroke:** Hot, dry, flushed skin — sweating may have stopped; common in elderly or sedentary individuals
- **Exertional heat stroke:** May still be sweating heavily — occurs in athletes and people working hard in heat
- Symptoms: confusion, slurred speech, loss of consciousness, seizure, rapid strong pulse

## Heat Stroke Treatment

- Call for emergency evacuation immediately — this is a true emergency
- Cool aggressively while waiting — every minute of elevated temperature causes organ damage
- Best method: immerse in cold or ice water if available
- Otherwise: wet clothing and fan vigorously; apply ice packs to neck, armpits, and groin
- Do not give fluids to an unconscious person

## Prevention

- Allow 10–14 days to acclimatize to hot conditions before intense exertion
- Drink before you feel thirsty — 16–32 oz of water per hour during moderate exertion in heat
- Schedule demanding activities for early morning or evening
- Dark yellow or brown urine indicates dehydration — increase fluid intake immediately

**In heat stroke, cooling is more important than evacuation — cool first, then move.**
